---
title: "Rmarkdown Holland & Patskowsky notes"
author: "Sidney"
date: "2024-12-17"
output: html_document
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

## R Markdown

This is an R Markdown document. Markdown is a simple formatting syntax for authoring HTML, PDF, and MS Word documents. For more details on using R Markdown see <http://rmarkdown.rstudio.com>.

When you click the **Knit** button a document will be generated that includes both content as well as the output of any embedded R code chunks within the document. You can embed an R code chunk like this:

```{r cars}
summary(cars)
```

## Including Plots

You can also embed plots, for example:

```{r pressure, echo=FALSE}
plot(pressure)
```

Note that the `echo = FALSE` parameter was added to the code chunk to prevent printing of the R code that generated the plot.

**Introduction summaries of different papers**
This markdown contains a summery of introductions from several papers to use for my own intro. For Holland & Patzkowsky 2015, I go deeper on how they explain clusters of last occurrences in different settings and summerise each mass extinction.

*Holland & Patzkowsky 2015*
With the possible exception of the end-Cretaceous, mass extinctions in the
fossil record are characterized by clusters of last occurrences at these
sequence stratigraphical horizons. Although these clusters of last occurrences
may represent brief pulses of elevated extinction, they are equally likely to
form by stratigraphical processes during a protracted period (more than several
hundred thousand years) of elevated extinction rate.
We propose three tests for distinguishing pulses of extinction from clusters of
last occurrences produced by stratigraphical processes.
In 1984 Raup and Sepkoski found periodicity in mass extinctions. They could be
sudden, pulsed or gradual. Major evidence for which manner fits a mass
extinction is the last occurrence of species in stratigraphical columns (Huber
1986; Macellari 1986; Kauffman 1988; Marshall and Ward 1996). But soon it was
found that it is not so simple and that a last occurrence does not always
depict the time of extinction. This is partly due to the Signor-Lipps effect,
by which incomplete sampling causes abrupt extinction events to appear gradual
in the stratigraphical record (Signor & Lipps 1982). I’m guessing they don’t
sample enough to show how many species died in a short amount of time. Patterns
of Last occurrences are still plainly used to infer timing and patterns of mass
extinctions (Finney et al. 1999; Jin et al. 2000; Brookfield et al. 2003; Shen
et al. 2011; Yan et al. 2013; Wang et al. 2014). 

The pattern of last occurrences in the stratigraphical record lies more complex
than how it’s often inferred. It does not directly represent time of
extinction. Facies change, changes in the rate of sedimentation, and hiatuses
exert a control on the occurrence of fossils that frequently overwhelms that of
sampling. Changing sedimentation rate and erosion exert a surprising amount of
complexity facies and how well last occurrences represent time of extinction
(Holland and  Patzkowsky 1999). Distortion of the stratigraphical record
through change in rate of sedimentation and facies change causes pulses of
extinction to seem like one event. Prolonged increased rates of extinction may
seem to occur in several pulses.
They plot an arbitrary sea level change with third order and fourth order
fluctuations. Then they look at 3 mass extinction scenarios and how they are
portrayed in the stratigraphical record on a siliciclastic platform.
stratigraphical columns are made at different locations on this siliciclastic
platform.
The first scenario depicts a mass extinction during slow rise in sea level
during Lowstand system tract. Last occurrences are elevated throughout LST.
There is a steady decrease of extinction (downward smearing) after the peak of
extinction. However, there is no steady increase from the onset to the peak of
extinction. Rather, it stays relatively flat through this interval. The
downward smearing is simply the result of the Signor-Lipps effect: surfaces and
intervals of major facies change will accumulate last occurrences. Such
surfaces can give the impression of additional pulses of extinction prior to
the actual extinction. This is because of the following of the FSST where there
is low to no deposition.
They then go through the 5 main mass extinctions on earth and place them into
context of those 3 scenarios. The sea level change is the same in all
scenarios. The model runs for 8 My. The first sea level cycle should not be
used as the model is still getting its chape. The last few My should also not
be looked at to avoid end model bias. Because the model does not continue after
the run, the last occurrences accumulate near the end of the run. They plot the
number of last occurrences in the depth domain. 
That there was an increased extinction rate is often clear. The question lies
in what extinction scenarios are consistent with the observed stratigraphical
pattern of last occurrences. If we know the sea level and the pattern of last
occurrences at the time we know the extinction scenario.
Subaerial unconformity = hiatus causes clustering of last occurrences
Major flooding surface = deepening & stratigraphical condensation causes
clustering.

*Late Ordovician* 
One or two clusters of last occurrences. Single cluster found in
depositionally updip settings. Found at subaerial unconformities and flooding
surfaces. Pair of clusters is found in downdip settings. This has led people
to believe the Ordovician mass extinction was a two-phase event.
The first phase: deep to shallow water facies. Primarily effected nektonic and
planktonic forms.
The second phase: major flooding surface. Deep-water mudstones overlying
shallow-water carbonates. Developed on a subaerial unconformity. Primarily
affected conodonts and shallow-water benthic taxa.
Two clusters is consistent with a prolonged extinction event spanning the
FSST, LST and earliest TST before the end of the Ordovician. The first cluster
is then produced by the forced regression of FSST. Disappearance of deep-water
planktonic taxa. The second cluster would lie at the transgressive surface,
the first flooding surface following the LST. Disappearance of shallow-water
taxa.
In updip areas FSST, LST and early TST are all within the hiatus resulting in
one cluster under the sequence-bounding unconformity.

*Late Devonian*
Possibly just decreased origination instead of elevated extinction rate
(Bambach et al., 2004). Faunal changes in three episodes. Second one is the
largest. 
Second event: In updip areas, the event consists of a sequence-bounding
unconformity merged with a flooding surface. Shallow-water carbonate deposits
to deeper-water organic-rich mudstone and shale. In downdip areas, there are
two clusters. One at the base of the FSST, with a loss of deeper-water pelagic
species, and one at the transgressive surface at op the LST, with a loss of
shallower-water species. 
Overall, greater loss of shallow-water taxa. Most last occurrences are
clustered at a major flooding surface. Possible that extinction was more
protracted than previously thought.

*End Permian*
Associated with a series of major flooding surfaces within the TST in which
last occurrences are distributed. The interval below the flooding surfaces
contains at least two subaerial unconformities. The extinction horizon and the
P/T boundary appear to be major flooding surfaces. The boundary has been
described as a second extinction horizon. Basal Triassic strata are highly
condensed. It’s all very complex. But, last occurrences have probably been
clustered.
The extinction event takes place over a longer period in the TST than
generally thought. Facies control plays an important role in the occurrence of
faunas as some species (i.e. conodonts) reappear later in the section. 

*End Triassic*
The boundary is divided in two parts. The lower part is a FSST capped by a
subaerial unconformity. The upper part is the lowest part of the TST and
contains several flooding surfaces. This part also contains several debris
flows during the early TST.
The boundary lies within a narrow (~3 m) interval characterized by a subaerial
unconformity and major flooding surfaces. Meaning that many of the last
occurrences lie at particular surfaces as a result of facies changes, hiatuses
and stratigraphical condensation.
Taxa prefer different facies causing last occurrences lie at different
horizons. The expression of the extinction in outcrops likely seems more
punctuated as a result of stratigraphical architecture. This makes it hard to
estimate the duration of the extinction event. But it was likely protracted. 

**Zimmt et al., 2020**
Clusters of last occurrences in the fossil record are frequently used to infer
the pattern (single or multiple extinction pulses), timing (age), and tempo
(prolonged or abrupt) of extinction events.
The structure of the stratigraphic record controls the distribution of fossil
occurrences in a stratigraphic column (Signor & Lipps 1982; Holland 1995;
Marshall 1997) typically producing clusters of last occurrences at
sequence-stratigraphic surfaces owing to changes in the preserved environment
(facies effects), variable rates of sedimentation (condensation effects), and
the presence of hiatuses (unconformity effects) in the measured sections
(Holland & Patzkowsky 1999, 2015; Holland 2020).
This potentially causes a single-pulsed or prolonged extinction event to be
interpreted as a multiple phases or pulsed extinction event. The application
of sequence-stratigraphic principles to the fossil record provides a
predictive framework for identifying the locations of stratigraphically
generated clusters of last occurrences. Clusters of last-occurrences that are
generated stratigraphically rather than actual pulses of extinction can be
identified using sequence-stratigraphic principles. You describe them in the
context of facies change/shifts & sequence-stratigraphic architecture rather
than purely taking them at face value.
Sea level exerts control on stratigraphic architecture (Catuneanu 2006;
Patzkowsky & Holland 2012; Holland 1995, 2020). When an extinction event
coincides with a change in sea level, an elevated extinction rate can appear
differently, for instance as multiple clusters of last occurrences, in the
stratigraphic record than how the extinction event happened in real life. 
Case study: Late Ordovician mass extinction

**Holland 2022**
Numerical models have been instrumental in understanding the stratigraphic
paleobiology of marine systems, that is, how the structure of the
stratigraphic record controls the expression of the fossil record (Holland
1995, 2000, 2020; Holland and Patzkowsky 1999, 2002, 2015). 
These models also guide the sampling of the fossil record, so that
evolutionary and ecological patterns can be distinguished from patterns that
have a stratigraphic origin (Patzkowsky and Holland  2007; Ivany et al. 2009).
In this research Holland looks at nonmarine fossils through a fluvial
deposition profile allowing for the accumulation and erosion of nonmarine
fossils. Just like Holland & Patzkowsky 2015, this is done in a siliciclastic
profile. They also include eustatic sea level change to simulate a marine
profile and a coastal-plain profile.
